result:
The rain comes down like a question nobody asked, tapping against the glass with the persistence of someone who believes, truly believes, that if they just keep knocking, the door will open.

I am sitting at a kitchen table that isn't mine. It belongs to the woman who lives here, Margaret, though she hasn't lived anywhere in about four months now. Her daughter asked me to house-sit. "Just to keep the place warm," she said, which is a strange way to put it. Houses don't have body heat. They don't keep warm just because someone is breathing in the next room. But I understood what she meant. She meant: make it look like someone still cares about this place. Turn the lights on and off. Collect the mail. Let the neighbors see a shape moving behind the curtain so the house doesn't become a house that something happened to.

Margaret left behind a lot of ceramics. Little bowls and cups and figurines on every surface. A clay cat with one ear. A blue vase with a crack running down its side that someone tried to fix with gold resin—kintsugi, I think it's called. The Japanese art of repairing broken things by making the break the most beautiful part. I run my finger over the gold line sometimes. It's raised, just slightly, like a scar that healed proud.

There's something about other people's objects. They don't carry your memories, so they carry only their own weight, which is heavier in a way. A coffee mug in your own kitchen is just a mug. You know its weight, the chip on the rim, how it feels in your hand. But a coffee mug in a stranger's kitchen is a mystery. Did she drink from it every morning? Was it a gift? Is it always placed upside down on the drying rack because of some private logic you'll never be let into?

I've started talking to the cat. The clay one. I set him on the windowsill above the sink and I say things like, "Well, it's raining again," which is the kind of thing people say when they've been alone long enough to need confirmation that weather is still happening. The cat doesn't respond. His one remaining ear is pointed slightly upward, as though he's listening to something just beyond my range of hearing. Maybe he is. Maybe the dead have a frequency.

I don't actually believe that. But there's something about being alone in a dead woman's house that makes the membrane between the world you understand and the world you don't feel very thin. Like you could press your palm against the air and it would give, like pushing through a curtain into another room.

The rain lets up around three. The sky does that thing where it can't decide if it's finished, so it keeps spitting a little, half-hearted, the way you keep sighing after you've finished crying, your body not quite convinced the feeling is over.

I go outside to get the mail. The box is at the end of the walkway, and the walkway is made of flagstones that have shifted over the years so that walking on them is a kind of wobbling negotiation. Margaret must have known exactly where each stone dipped and tilted. She probably walked this path thousands of times, her body memorizing the geography of it the way a river memorizes a landscape, not with thought but with repeated yielding.

The mail is what it always is. Catalogs. Coupons. A postcard from someone named Dot who writes in tiny, slanted handwriting: *The azaleas are unreal this year. Wish you were here to see them. Love, Dot.* The postcard is from Savannah. The azaleas on the front are a kind of violent pink, almost too much, almost an accusation. I set it on the counter beside the clay cat and think about how strange it is to send a postcard to a house where the person is gone. The postcard doesn't know. It just does its job—picture, stamp, address—and trusts that whatever it finds on the other end will match what it expects.

I've been here two weeks now, and I've developed a routine. Coffee. Walk. Read. Eat something minimal. Watch the light change through the window over the sink, which faces west, so in the late afternoon everything goes golden and the gold resin on the broken vase catches the sun and throws a thin bright line across the ceiling like a laser. Then dark. Then the rain again, usually.

I keep thinking about kintsugi. About the idea that the breaking is not the end of the story, that the repair can be the most beautiful part. It's a lovely thought, but I wonder if it's true. I wonder if some cracks are too deep to fill with gold. I wonder if some things break in ways that no amount of careful, loving repair can make beautiful. Maybe they just become the thing they are—broken—and you learn to live with the brokenness, not because it's beautiful, but because it's what's left.

Margaret's daughter called yesterday. She asked how the house was. I said it was fine. She said, "That's good. That's really good." And then there was a silence between us, not unfriendly, just full of something unspoken, the way silence between strangers is full of all the things neither of you knows how to say.

I almost told her about the postcard from Dot. But it felt too private, somehow. Like reading someone else's letter, even though that's exactly what it was. The azaleas are unreal this year. I keep turning that sentence over. Unreal. As though the flowers had exceeded their own reality, become more than what flowers are supposed to be.

That's what grief does, I think. It makes the world unreal. Too bright, too much, too vivid in a way that hurts. Or maybe it's the opposite. Maybe it makes the world less than it was. Maybe unreal means a kind of fading.

The light is going now. The gold line on the ceiling has disappeared. The clay cat sits on the windowsill with his one good ear, listening. Outside, the rain has started again, tapping its question against the glass.

I don't have an answer.

But I'm here. And the house is warm.

prompt: You have 1000 words. Write whatever comes to you.
